figures.gloss: The Sun herself, life-giver and ripener, who returns over the rim of the polar dark and to whom white reindeer were sacrificed, their meat threaded on rings of birch-twig wound sunwise; in the deepest winter she is implored to bring back the green and the fertility of the herds. On the drumheads she rides at the very centre as a great rayed lozenge.
figures.facet: A sun worshipped against the polar night, not the desert glare: in a land of months-long darkness the Sun is not a sovereign to be feared but a returning warmth begged back with white-reindeer blood and sunwise birch — solar divinity figured as the survival of light, and most often felt as feminine.
figures.provenance: Drum iconography (the central rayed figure on countless runebommer) plus 17th–18th-c. missionary records; Beaivi's gender wavers across regions in the sources, sometimes solar mother, sometimes paired with a moon-spouse.

figures.gloss: The Moon, lamp of the long winter and reckoner of the dark half of the year, set opposite Beaivi the Sun and like her given a place on the sacred drum; the night-light by which the herder reads the season when the sun has sunk below the world's edge for weeks together.
figures.facet: The Moon as the OTHER half of an Arctic year split in two: where milder peoples mark day and night, Sápmi is governed by a sun-season and a moon-season, and Mánnu is the ruling light of the dark months — the lunar joint reached as a calendar of polar survival, not a mere nocturnal counterpart.
figures.provenance: Attested on the drums opposite the solar figure and in the missionary god-lists; the lunar cult is thinner in the record than the solar, often known chiefly through the drum's paired sun-and-moon symbols.

figures.gloss: The thunder-god who rules weather, life and death, carrying a hammer (the vietjer) and sometimes two, who hurls the bolt, splits the troll-rock and is figured on the drum gripping his mallet; called Tiermes or Dierpmis in the north and Horagalles in the south, he is implored for fair weather and to smite the unseen evil powers.
figures.facet: A Thor in NAME yet not in blood: 'Horagalles' is the Norse 'Þórr karl' (Thor the old man / Thor-fellow) taken up by a Uralic people from their Norse neighbours, the borrowed title laid over an indigenous thunder-power (Tiermes/Dierpmis) — the rare case where the gold seam of a name is genuinely documented across the IE/Uralic line.
figures.provenance: The Horagalles = *Þórr-karl etymology is a long-standing and well-supported reading of the missionary sources (Friis, Læstadius, the 17th–18th-c. manuscripts); the native northern names Tiermes/Dierpmis show the thunder-god predates and underlies the imported Norse title.

figures.gloss: The 'Alder-Man', lord of the forest and master of its game, ruler above all of the bear, who must be propitiated before the hunt and who grants or withholds the quarry; alder-bark, chewed to a blood-red dye, was spat or sprinkled on hunters, weapons and the slain bear in his rites, the red of the alder standing for the blood of the chase.
figures.facet: The keeper of the wild named for the ALDER, the tree that 'bleeds' red when cut: the master-of-animals is fixed here to one species whose dye reddens every rite of the hunt, so that the lord of the game is met through the bleeding bark of a single sacred tree — ecology condensed into one plant's wound.
figures.provenance: Recorded by the 18th-c. missionary-ethnographers (notably the alder-bark/red-dye bear rites); Leib-olmai's name and forest-and-bear lordship are well attested, though his exact rank among the spirits varies by source.

figures.gloss: The mother-goddess Máttaráhkká and her three daughters, the Áhkká, who dwell beneath the floor of the lavvu and govern birth: Sáráhkká, nearest the hearth, opens the womb and shares the mother's birth-pangs, receiving offerings in the fire and food; Juoksáhkká, the bow-woman, can turn a girl-child to a boy; Uksáhkká at the door wards the newborn's first steps. Máttaráhkká takes the soul and gives it a body — most commonly said to come from Beaivi the Sun, though some regional and missionary sources instead have it descend from Radien-attje.
figures.facet: A pantheon of WOMEN beneath the tent-floor running the whole work of birth — conception, sex-of-the-child, delivery, the threshold — addressed by the household's women as the daily, ground-level divinity: a female-centred, domestic, sub-floor godhead that is the living centre of Sámi religion where the sky-gods are remote and occasional.
figures.provenance: The Áhkká complex is among the best-attested Sámi cults (Læstadius's 'Fragments of Lappish Mythology', the 17th–18th-c. manuscripts, and Bäckman & Hultkrantz); their place beneath the lavvu floor and the division of birth-offices is consistently reported.

figures.gloss: The death-mother who rules Jábmeaimo, the underworld of the dead beneath the living earth, where the deceased lead a second, shadowed life; she holds the dead and can claim the sick toward her realm, and the noaidi must descend and bargain — often with the sacrifice of a black animal — to redeem a soul she is drawing down.
figures.facet: An underworld ruled by a MOTHER, mirroring the birth-mothers above: as the Áhkká beneath the tent-floor give the body, Jábmeáhkká beneath the earth receives it — death imagined as a return to a maternal keeping under the ground, the female sovereignty of Sámi belief carried clean through into the land of the dead.
figures.provenance: Recorded in the missionary sources and synthesised in Bäckman & Hultkrantz; Jábmeaimo and its mistress belong to a layered Sámi afterworld (with the lower Rota-aimo), the details varying across the manuscripts.

figures.gloss: The Sámi shaman, who beats the goavddis (the runebomme drum) into trance and, lying as if dead, sends his free-soul out in the shape of a fish, a bird or a reindeer to travel the worlds — diving to Jábmeaimo to ransom a sick soul, racing rival noaidis as fighting bull-reindeer, reading the future from the brass ring that dances over the painted drumhead as the hammer falls.
figures.facet: A soul-traveller whose vehicle is the herd-and-water world itself: the noaidi flies not as an abstract spirit but AS a fish through the lakes, a bird through the air, a bull-reindeer across the fells — and noaidi-duels are fought in those animal forms, the ecstatic journey wholly bodied in the fauna of Sápmi.
figures.provenance: Among the most fully recorded shamanisms in Europe — drum-trance, the arpa (the ring/index on the drum), and the animal-form soul-flight are detailed in the 17th–18th-c. accounts and in Bäckman & Hultkrantz; many drums were confiscated and burned in the Christianisation, so the record is also the record of a suppression.

figures.gloss: The shaman's drum, an oval or bowl of birch or pine roofed with a reindeer-hide head painted in alder-red with a cosmos: sun and moon, the gods, the world's three or more tiers, the herds and the holy places. The brass arpa-ring set on it leaps from figure to figure as the drum is struck — at once the map of the worlds, the instrument of trance, and the oracle read by where the ring comes to rest.
figures.facet: The ONLY map of the three worlds the Sámi drew: cosmology here is not spoken in epic but PAINTED on a drumhead, a single membrane on which heaven, earth and the dead are laid out and then read like a board — the world-axis and the world-picture fused into one struck object, divination and diagram in the same skin.
figures.provenance: Some seventy painted drums survive in museums, seized as idols by missionaries; their layouts are the primary visual source for Sámi cosmology, read alongside the manuscript accounts and the syntheses of Bäckman & Hultkrantz and of Manker's drum catalogue.

figures.gloss: A sacred site — most often a strangely shaped stone or rock, sometimes a stump or a cleft — that is itself a power and a place of offering: smeared with the fat and blood of reindeer, antlers heaped before it, given the first of the catch, and bound to a family or a fishing-water whose luck it holds. To feed the sieidi well is to keep the game and the fish coming.
figures.facet: Divinity LODGED in a particular stone on a particular shore — not a temple raised by hands but a found feature of the land recognised as holy, the sacred read off the terrain itself; a religion of named places rather than built shrines, where the rock IS the god and the offering is its meal.
figures.provenance: Sieidi sites are attested archaeologically (bone, antler and metal-offering deposits) as well as in the missionary record; they remained in quiet use long after formal conversion, and many are still known on the land in Sápmi today.

figures.gloss: The great hunting rite: the bear, slain in its winter den, is addressed with apology and honorific by-names so its spirit takes no offence, its body brought home with song and ceremony, the flesh eaten by men and women apart under strict rule, and the bones — above all the skull — buried whole in anatomical order in a bear-grave so the lord of the forest may clothe them in flesh again and the bear return to be hunted anew.
figures.facet: Killing performed as an APOLOGY and a promise of resurrection: the most powerful and most human-like beast is hunted only with elaborate courtesy and then re-assembled in the ground so it can come back — the hunter's debt to the animal he eats made into a liturgy of respect and renewal, the bear honoured precisely as it is killed.
figures.provenance: The Sámi bear-feast is recorded in 17th–18th-c. accounts and confirmed by excavated ordered bear-graves; it belongs to a circumpolar bear-ceremonialism (compared by Hallowell, and synthesised for Sápmi in Bäckman & Hultkrantz), shared in pattern across the northern hunting peoples.

figures.gloss: The heavenly 'Ruler' standing at the top of the South-Sámi drum's god-row, alongside his consort Radien-akka and son Radien-pardne; in several missionary-recorded tellings he, rather than Beaivi, releases the soul of the child-to-be downward toward Máttaráhkká to be clothed in a body.
figures.facet: A high god reached almost entirely through 18th-century missionary transcription rather than living cult: Radien-attje sits above the working, addressed powers (Beaivi, the Áhkká, the noaidi's spirits) as a remote authority whose main act is releasing the soul at the very top of a chain others actually administer — sovereignty as origin-point, not as object of daily prayer.
figures.provenance: South-Sámi missionary god-lists of the 17th-18th c. (synthesised in Bäckman & Hultkrantz); the name's transparent Swedish loan ('Radien' from Swedish 'regera', to rule) marks this as a title shaped under Scandinavian contact rather than a purely indigenous term.

figures.gloss: The world-sustaining god of fertility and increase, well attested in the southern Sámi sources, honored with a world-pillar (veralden-čuovga or similar sacrificial post) raised at sacrificial sites to physically hold up the sky.
figures.facet: Cosmic support rendered as a literal, plantable object: rather than a myth of the sky being propped up in illo tempore, the Sámi raise an actual pillar at the actual sacrificial site, so that the axis-mundi is renewed by ritual action each time it is set up, not merely inherited from a single founding act.
figures.provenance: Southern Sámi missionary sources on Veralden Olmai and the world-pillar rite; scholars (following the 'veraldar guð' title borne by the Norse god Freyr in Icelandic sources) have long connected the name to Norse fertility-kingship vocabulary, though the exact channel of contact is inferred rather than documented in a surviving Sámi-side text.

figures.gloss: The wind-god, who shovels the gales out of his mountain cave with two shovels, implored by herders and fishers for calm weather on the fells and the lakes; a standard member of the recorded drum god-lists.
figures.facet: Weather divided by department rather than concentrated in one storm-lord: where Horagalles governs the thunderbolt, Bieggolmai governs the wind alone, so that the Sámi pantheon answers the Arctic's changeable weather with a specialist for each hazard rather than a single all-purpose sky-power.
figures.provenance: Recorded in the 17th-18th-c. missionary god-lists alongside Horagalles and Beaivi; the two-shovels detail and the cave are a standard element of the recorded folklore.

figures.gloss: The power of plague and death, propitiated by being symbolically ridden away from the community on a sacrificed horse and banished down to Rota-aimo, the deepest tier of the land of the dead beneath Jábmeáhkká's own realm.
figures.facet: Disease made rideable: rather than a plague simply striking a community, Ruto is treated as an entity that can be mounted on a horse and sent off in a direction, turning epidemic sickness into a problem of banishment and geography rather than of medicine — plague answered with a journey, not a cure.
figures.provenance: Recorded in the missionary sources and synthesised in Bäckman & Hultkrantz; the name's resemblance to Old Norse vocabulary for plague or to a borrowed Scandinavian death-figure is noted in the literature but not conclusively settled, and is flagged here rather than asserted.

figures.gloss: The bright underworld of the happy dead, located beneath sacred lakes and fells rather than beneath the earth generally; its inhabitants — the sáivu-gadže (people), and the sáivu fish, bird, and reindeer — serve the noaidi as spirit-helpers and mounts for the soul-journey.
figures.facet: A second, GOOD land of the dead standing opposite Jábmeaimo's grim one: Sámi cosmology does not send every soul to the same fate, but splits the dead between a feared underworld of general shades and a blessed, lake-bound otherworld whose inhabitants actively assist the living through the shaman — the afterlife itself divided by function, not just by merit.
figures.provenance: Attested across the 17th-18th-c. missionary manuscripts and standard in the synthesis of Bäckman & Hultkrantz; specific sáivu sites (lakes and fells believed to house it) are named in several regional traditions.

figures.gloss: The huge, rich, slow-witted ogre of the winter fells in Sámi folktale, who challenges people to duels, tries to steal or eat them, and is regularly outwitted rather than outfought — his wealth (cattle, silver) often becomes the human hero's prize.
figures.facet: A figure of folklore rather than of cult: unlike the deities and spirits addressed in actual rite and sacrifice, Stállu belongs to the told tale, not the offering — the tradition's own space for entertainment, moral instruction, and (many scholars note) an imagined caricature of hostile outsiders, kept separate from the working religious system around him.
figures.provenance: Widely recorded in Sámi oral folktale collections across the 19th-20th c.; consistently a narrative antagonist rather than an object of sacrifice or prayer, distinguishing him from the drum-attested deities in this roster.

edges.note: Sun and Moon stand opposite each other on the drumheads as the paired lights of the Arctic year — Beaivi ruling the sun-season, Mánnu the months of polar dark.
Máttaráhkká takes the new soul down from Beaivi the Sun and clothes it in a body — the solar life-giver and the birth-mothers form one chain from light to flesh.

Mirror-mothers at opposite ends of life: the Áhkká beneath the tent-floor give the body at birth, Jábmeáhkká beneath the earth receives it at death — the same maternal sovereignty turned from cradle to grave.
The noaidi cannot travel the worlds without the drum: the goavddis is both the instrument that beats him into trance and the painted map his free-soul reads — shaman and drum are one working tool.
The noaidi's hardest journey is the descent to Jábmeaimo to bargain back a soul the death-mother is drawing down — psychopomp and underworld-sovereign locked in the same rite.
Leib-olmai the Alder-Man is the lord above all of the bear; his red alder-dye reddens the bear-rites and it is to him the ordered bones are given so the bear may be re-fleshed and return.
The two surfaces of Sámi sacred space — the cosmos painted on the drumhead and the cosmos found in the holy stone; the drum often maps the very sieidi-sites it is read beside.

The strong gold here: 'Horagalles' is the Norse 'Þórr karl' (Thor the old man) borrowed by the Sámi from their Norse neighbours and laid over the native thunder-god — a documented name-borrowing across the Indo-European/Uralic line, the title imported even as Tiermes/Dierpmis stays indigenous.

Hammer-and-bolt thunder-gods of the northern forests who smite the powers of evil; a structural rhyme with the Slavic storm-father, but Perun's *Perkwunos name leaves no Uralic descendant — the pattern shared, not the blood.

The thunder-wielder who shatters the rock and looses the waters/weather is one of humanity's most widespread divine offices; Tiermes meets Indra at the storm-wielder joint by independent road, no descent between Uralic and Indo-Aryan.

Two Uralic thunder-'old-men' of Fennoscandia, the Sámi Horagalles/Tiermes and the Finnish Ukko, sharing hammer, bolt and bow through close Finnic-Sámi neighbourhood — kindred storm-fathers reached without the Indo-European word.

The Sámi noaidi and the Siberian shaman are two members of one great North-Eurasian shamanism: drum-trance, the three worlds, the soul sent out and the descent to ransom the sick — a real circumpolar pattern, shared inheritance or deep diffusion rather than documented contact.

Both the Arctic soul-traveller who leaves the body to fly the worlds and treat with the powers of game and death; the noaidi diverges by riding the herd-world itself (fish, bird, bull-reindeer), where the angakkuq descends to the sea-mother of the seals.
Both ecstatic specialists who treat the living by dealing with spirits and the dead; a loose North-Asian shamanic rhyme across utterly separate peoples, flagged as a soft parallel.

The drum is the Sámi axis-mundi made portable — where the Siberian shaman climbs the world-tree, the noaidi reads the three worlds off the painted membrane he strikes; the same vertical cosmos held in different objects.
Leib-olmai is the Sámi instance of the keeper who owns the wild and looses the game — the master-of-animals joint, fixed here to the alder-tree and the bear above all other quarry.

Forest-lords of neighbouring Uralic peoples who hold the keys to the game and must be coaxed before the bow finds its mark — the Sámi Alder-Man and the Finnish green king Tapio, kindred keepers of the woods across the Finnic-Sámi border.
Both preside over the wild and its beasts and govern the hunt's right conduct; an honest structural parallel of the wilderness-keeper, no descent or contact between Sámi and Greek.

Two northern reverences for the bear as the near-human beast — the Sámi honour and re-bury it to return, the Korean Ungnyeo is the she-bear who becomes a woman and ancestress; the sacred kinship of bear and human, on a circumpolar belt with no contact.

The bear treated with apology and re-fleshed so the game returns rhymes with the Inuit master of the land-animals who must be kept in good will for the hunt to renew — the hunter's debt to the owned wild, met across the Arctic by separate peoples.

The ordered burial of the bear's bones so the lord of the forest may clothe them in flesh again sets this rite at the dying-rising joint — resurrection figured not for a god but for the honoured prey, killed only to come back.

Northern solar divinities reached against the long winter, both frequently feminine — Beaivi the returning Sámi Sun and the Baltic sun-goddess Saulė; the solar joint filled by the warmth begged back over the dark, in two unrelated families of the same latitudes.

Both the Sun personified and given the central place in cult and image; a thin parallel, since Beaivi diverges as an often-feminine warmth implored back from the polar night rather than a charioteer crossing a Mediterranean sky — flagged as loose.

Bleak northern lands of the dead beneath the world ruled by a death-keeping power; the Sámi Jábmeaimo and the Finnish Tuonela rhyme as sober Uralic afterworlds of shadowed second life, by Finnic-Sámi neighbourhood rather than descent.

An underworld of the dead ruled by a female sovereign — Jábmeáhkká and the Norse Hel both women who keep the departed; an analogous death-mistress across the Uralic/Germanic line, contact plausible but the parallel structural.

Household birth-goddesses who govern conception and safe delivery from within the dwelling — the Sámi Áhkká beneath the tent-floor and the Korean Samsin grandmother of the inner room; the domestic, female sovereignty of birth, convergent across Asia's north.

The near, warm mother-power of birth complementing a remote sky — Máttaráhkká's household of birth-mothers beside the Inner-Asian womb-goddess Umai; the great-mother met at the threshold of the cradle, no descent claimed.

Sacred stones recognised as holy and made the focus of offering and pilgrimage; the Sámi offering-rock and the Black Stone are far-flung, unrelated answers to the impulse to find godhead lodged in stone — a distant rhyme, flagged.

Moon-gods of the northern latitudes paired against the Sun — the Sámi Mánnu and the Baltic Mēnulis; the lunar joint as the ruling light of the dark season, reached in two separate families along the same cold sky.

Medieval Norse sources themselves link seiðr and Sámi noaidi practice through recorded contact and intermarriage of magic-workers in the north (e.g. Vatnsdæla saga); the connection is attested historically, not merely a modern structural echo, though the exact direction of influence is debated by scholars such as Neil Price.

The bear as the circumpolar world's paradigmatic ritual animal-ally recurs independently: Nanurluk's initiatory power to Greenlandic angakkuit rhymes with the Sami bear cult's ceremonial treatment of the slain bear, though the Sami rite centers an ordinary bear's ritual death-and-return while Nanurluk is a singular monstrous tuurngaq.

Cross-tradition: unrelated circumpolar shamanic specialists whose regalia is treated as independently charged with power and whose office is to travel to, or treat with, the spirit world for the community.

In several South-Sámi god-row tellings the newborn soul descends from Radien-attje, not Beaivi, to Máttaráhkká to be clothed in a body — a regional variant of the same soul-chain the Beaivi/Máttaráhkká pairing already records in this roster.

Two weather-powers of the drum god-rows dividing the sky's violence between them: Horagalles wields the thunderbolt, Bieggolmai the gale — specialist storm-powers rather than one god's whole portfolio.
Ruto's realm, Rota-aimo, is the deepest tier of Jábmeáhkká's own underworld — the plague-power's domain nested inside, not separate from, the death-mother's.
Banishing Ruto's plague by riding it away on a sacrificed horse down to Rota-aimo is, alongside ransoming souls from Jábmeáhkká, one of the noaidi's great ritual tasks.

Sáivu's inhabitants (the sáivu-gadže and the sáivu fish, bird, and reindeer) are the noaidi's own spirit-helpers and soul-vehicles — without this blessed otherworld the shaman's helping spirits have no home to be summoned from.

The Sámi afterlife splits in two: Jábmeaimo beneath the earth, general and grim, ruled by a death-mother; Sáivu beneath sacred lakes and fells, blessed and helpful, its dead serving the living through the shaman — the same underworld impulse divided into a feared half and a beneficent half.

Where the noaidi's power is real cult, addressed with drum and sacrifice, Stállu's is told power, met only in the tale — the roster's clearest boundary between the working religious system and its surrounding folklore.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: stone-cairn/rock cult at outdoor sacred sites, circled and given offerings by travelers — a widespread Eurasian pattern of the local numen made of stone, not shared descent.

dossier.essence: The pre-Christian and folk religion of the Sámi, the indigenous people of Sápmi (northern Norway, Sweden, Finland, and Russia's Kola Peninsula) — a hunting, fishing, and reindeer-herding religion of the European Arctic. It centered on a tiered spirit-world, a set of deities of sun, thunder, birth, and death, sacred natural sites (sieidi), the bear cult, and the drum-using shaman (noaidi). Heavily suppressed by Lutheran missions in the 17th–18th centuries, it is the subject of a contemporary cultural and spiritual revival.
dossier.cosmos: The world has tiers — sky-deities above, the living in the middle, and an underworld of the dead (Jábmeaimo) ruled by the death-mother Jábmeáhkká. Its powers include Beaivi the Sun and Mánnu the Moon, the thunder-god (Tiermes/Dierpmis, southern Horagalles), the game-lord Leib-olmai, and the birth-goddess Máttaráhkká with her daughters, who govern conception and delivery beneath the tent floor. The soul, drawn from the sun-realm and given a body by the birth-mothers, passes at death into the underworld's shadowed second life, from which the noaidi may bargain an endangered soul back.
dossier.practice: Offerings of reindeer fat, blood, and antlers were made at sieidi — sacred stones and natural features tied to a family's hunting or fishing luck. The bear-feast honored the slain bear with apology and buried its bones in order so that it might return. The noaidi beat the goavddis (runebomme), a reindeer-hide drum painted with a map of the worlds, into trance and read a brass ring's movements across it for divination, while the household birth-rites of the Áhkká goddesses were largely the women's domain.
dossier.canon: No scripture — an oral religion. The record is the surviving painted drums (some seventy in museums), the archaeology of sieidi offering-sites, and the 17th–18th-century accounts of Lutheran missionaries (behind Knud Leem and Lars Levi Læstadius's 'Fragments of Lappish Mythology'), synthesized in modern scholarship.
dossier.reflects: Cosmology drawn and struck rather than written — a shamanic, female-centered Arctic religion in which the sacred is mapped on a drumhead and lodged in specific stones of the land.
sources: Louise Bäckman and Åke Hultkrantz, 'Studies in Lapp Shamanism' (1978)
Håkan Rydving, 'The End of Drum-Time: Religious Change among the Lule Saami, 1670s–1740s' (1993)
